title: Dot Product Similarity
concept: How dot product folds in vector magnitude and when normalized embeddings make it equivalent to cosine.

exercises.prompt: In the last lesson, we learned that Cosine Similarity measures which of the following?

options.text: The angle between vectors, ignoring magnitude.
The straight-line distance between two vector endpoints.
The product of the lengths of the vectors.

exercises.explain: Cosine similarity measures the angle between two vectors, completely ignoring their length or magnitude. This makes it great for comparing text documents of different lengths.

cards.title: Beyond Just Angles
cards.body: We know cosine similarity measures the angle between vectors and ignores their size. But what if the size of the vector actually contains important information, like how often a word appears or how detailed a profile is? That is where the dot product comes in.

cards.title: What is Dot Product?
cards.body: Dot product similarity multiplies corresponding coordinates of two vectors and sums them up. Unlike cosine similarity, it cares about both the direction and the length (magnitude) of the vectors.

cards.title: The Searchlight Beam
cards.body: Imagine two searchlights. Cosine similarity only checks if they are pointing in the exact same direction. Dot product checks if they point in the same direction AND multiplies how bright (long) each light beam is!

cards.title: Length Matters
cards.body: If Vector A represents a short article [1, 2] and Vector B represents a long article [3, 6], they point in the exact same direction. Their dot product is large because the numbers are large, even though their cosine similarity is a perfect 1.0.

cards.title: Is Bigger Always Better?
cards.body: You might think dot product is always superior because it has more information. Actually, if your data has varying text lengths, raw dot product can unfairly favor long, wordy documents just because they have larger vector values.

cards.title: Cosine vs. Dot Product
cards.body: See how changing vector length changes the dot product, while the cosine similarity remains locked onto the direction.

left.text: Ignores vector length; only measures the angular difference.

right.text: Multiplies direction alignment by the magnitudes of both vectors.

exercises.prompt: Under what condition does dot product similarity behave exactly like cosine similarity?

options.text: When the vectors are extremely long.
When the vectors are orthogonal (at 90 degrees).
When the vectors are normalized to have a magnitude of 1.
When the vectors have only negative coordinates.

exercises.explain: If all vectors in a database are normalized (scaled to a length of exactly 1.0), then the magnitude factor disappears from the dot product equation, making it mathematically identical to cosine similarity.

exercises.prompt: True or False: If two vectors point in the exact same direction, increasing the length of one vector will increase their dot product similarity.
exercises.explain: True! Dot product multiplies the components and sums them. Larger vector values (magnitudes) will result in a larger dot product, even if the angle remains identical.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to describe what dot product includes that cosine similarity ignores:
exercises.sentence: The dot product is affected by both vector direction and vector magnitude.
exercises.explain: The dot product combines both the angle (direction) and the magnitude (length) of the two vectors, unlike cosine similarity which only looks at the angle.

exercises.prompt: Identify which response is written by an AI assistant explaining normalized dot product.

options.text: I usually just normalize my vectors because it makes dot product run way faster on my GPU clusters.
In my coding class, we learned that normalizing vectors means we don't have to worry about cosine division anymore.
When vectors are normalized to unit length, their magnitude is 1. Consequently, the dot product simplifies to the cosine of the angle between them.

exercises.explain: Option C is a typical structured response from an AI assistant explaining the mathematical equivalence between dot product and cosine similarity for unit vectors. Options A and B are written from a conversational personal perspective.

exercises.prompt: Why does using 'normalized embeddings' make Dot Product similarity give the exact same ranking results as Cosine Similarity?

options.text: Dot product ignores magnitude entirely, which makes it slow.
Normalizing vectors sets their magnitudes to 1, simplifying the dot product to just the cosine of the angle.
Cosine similarity is always larger than dot product for normalized vectors.
Normalized vectors double the speed of calculations but yield different ranking orders.

exercises.explain: When embeddings are normalized, their lengths (magnitudes) are exactly 1. Because the dot product formula is (magnitude A) * (magnitude B) * (cosine of angle), having magnitudes of 1 reduces the formula to just 1 * 1 * (cosine of angle), making it identical to Cosine Similarity.
